agent_output:
## Domain Analysis — Driver Earnings Transparency

### Business Context

This feature encodes the business process of **compensation disclosure to drivers** — surfacing how each delivery's earnings are composed (base pay, bonuses, tips, deductions) in real time. This is not merely a UI feature; it is a **regulated compensation process** that intersects labor law, worker classification, and institutional approval rights across jurisdictions. Getting this wrong doesn't just break UX — it creates legal liability, regulatory fines, and labor disputes.

---

### Ubiquitous Language

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Base Pay** | The guaranteed minimum compensation per delivery or per time unit, determined by worker classification and jurisdiction. For employees (CA), this must meet or exceed minimum wage. For contractors (Indonesia), this is the agreed contract rate. |
| **Per-Delivery Bonus** | Variable compensation tied to completing a delivery, distinct from base pay. May include surge pricing, distance premiums, or performance incentives. |
| **Tip** | Voluntary payment from customer to driver. **Must never be commingled with base pay or used to satisfy minimum wage obligations** (CA law). |
| **Deduction** | Any amount subtracted from gross earnings — equipment fees, insurance, platform fees, tax withholdings. Each deduction must be individually itemized and legally authorized. |
| **Earnings Breakdown** | The complete, itemized view of a single pay period or delivery showing all four components and the net result. |
| **Worker Classification** | The legal status of the driver — employee vs. independent contractor — which determines which compensation rules apply. This is **jurisdiction-bound**, not platform-wide. |
| **Works Council (Betriebsrat)** | German employee representative body with **co-determination rights** over compensation display systems. Has legal blocking power — not advisory. |
| **Real-Time** | Earnings data reflects the current state within minutes of a delivery event, not batch-processed at end of pay period. |

### Bounded Context

**Driver Compensation / Earnings Visibility** — sits at the intersection of the Payroll, Delivery, and Compliance bounded contexts. It reads from payroll calculations but is owned by the Driver Experience domain. Critically, the **rules governing what must/can/cannot be shown are owned by the Compliance context**, not the UI team.

---

### Business Rules

| # | Rule | Invariant? | Testable Assertion |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BR-1** | Tips must never be included in or offset against base pay calculations in California. | ✅ YES | `Given a CA delivery, When base pay is calculated, Then tip amount has zero effect on the base pay figure.` |
| **BR-2** | In California, the earnings breakdown MUST be displayed to every driver. This is not optional or configurable. | ✅ YES | `Given a CA-classified driver, When they complete a delivery, Then an itemized earnings breakdown is visible without requiring opt-in.` |
| **BR-3** | In Germany, the earnings display feature MUST NOT go live until works council approval is formally recorded. | ✅ YES | `Given the Germany jurisdiction, When works council approval status = NOT_APPROVED, Then the earnings breakdown feature is disabled for all DE drivers.` |
| **BR-4** | Every deduction must be individually itemized with a reason code and legal authorization reference. No "miscellaneous" or lump-sum deductions. | ✅ YES | `Given any earnings breakdown, When deductions are present, Then each deduction has a non-empty reason code AND authorization reference.` |
| **BR-5** | California drivers classified as employees under AB5 must see that their effective hourly rate meets or exceeds the applicable minimum wage. | ✅ YES | `Given a CA employee driver, When viewing earnings for a pay period, Then effective_hourly_rate >= CA_minimum_wage for that period.` |
| **BR-6** | In Indonesia, the platform MAY show earnings breakdown but is not legally required to. If shown, it must accurately reflect the contractor agreement terms. | ❌ No | `Given an ID contractor driver, When earnings are displayed, Then all figures match the terms in the active contractor agreement.` |
| **BR-7** | Worker classification determines which rule set applies. A driver's classification is jurisdiction-bound and must never be inferred or defaulted — it must be explicitly set per jurisdiction. | ✅ YES | `Given a driver, When earnings rules are evaluated, Then worker_classification is explicitly set (not null/default) AND matches the jurisdiction's legal framework.` |
| **BR-8** | Earnings data must be available in the driver's local currency with no ambiguity about currency denomination. | ✅ YES | `Given any earnings breakdown, Then currency_code is explicitly displayed AND matches the driver's operating jurisdiction.` |
| **BR-9** | The breakdown must show gross earnings, each deduction line, and net earnings. `net = gross - sum(deductions)` must always hold. | ✅ YES | `Given any earnings breakdown, Then net_earnings == gross_earnings - sum(all_deduction_amounts), with zero tolerance.` |
| **BR-10** | Historical earnings data must be immutable once a pay period is finalized. Corrections must appear as separate adjustment line items, not mutations. | ✅ YES | `Given a finalized pay period, When an earnings correction is needed, Then a new adjustment line item is created AND original line items remain unchanged.` |

---

### Business Impact

**Financial:**
- **California non-compliance** carries penalties of $100/driver/pay period for first violation, $200+ for subsequent violations under LWDA enforcement, plus class-action exposure. A fleet of 10,000 CA drivers with a single systemic violation could mean $1M+ in the first pay period alone.
- Accurate, transparent earnings can **reduce driver disputes and payment-related support tickets** by 30-50% based on industry benchmarks.
- Getting Germany wrong (deploying without works council approval) can trigger injunctions and fines, plus damages to the employment relationship.

**Operational:**
- Requires a **jurisdiction-aware rules engine** — not just i18n/l10n, but fundamentally different business logic per region. This is not a flag toggle; it's a compliance architecture.
- Real-time calculation adds load to payroll services. Must handle delivery volume spikes without stale data.
- Germany's works council process has **no guaranteed timeline** — the feature rollout must be decoupled per jurisdiction, not a global big-bang.

**User (Driver) Impact:**
- High positive impact on driver trust and retention. Earnings opacity is the #1 driver complaint across gig platforms.
- In Indonesia, even though not required, offering transparency is a competitive differentiator for driver recruitment.

**Risk:**
- **Highest risk**: Shipping California without full compliance, or shipping Germany without works council sign-off. Both create immediate legal exposure.
- **Data accuracy risk**: If the breakdown shows incorrect figures, it becomes evidence against the platform in disputes. The feature creates a **permanent audit trail** — wrong numbers are worse than no numbers.
- **Classification risk**: If the system accidentally applies employee rules to Indonesian contractors (or vice versa), it could be used as evidence of misclassification.

**Stakeholders Affected:**

| Stakeholder | Impact |
|---|---|
| **Drivers (CA)** | Legally entitled to this information; directly affects trust and retention |
| **Drivers (DE)** | Cannot receive feature until works council approves; must be communicated clearly |
| **Drivers (ID)** | Positive UX improvement, no legal obligation |
| **Legal / Compliance** | Must validate rules per jurisdiction before launch; ongoing audit responsibility |
| **Works Council (DE)** | Has co-determination right; must be engaged as a partner, not informed after the fact |
| **Finance / Payroll** | Source of truth for all earnings data; must guarantee accuracy of real-time feed |
| **Product / Engineering** | Must build jurisdiction-aware architecture, not a one-size-fits-all feature |
| **Customer Support** | Expect reduced ticket volume for pay disputes post-launch |

---

### Acceptance Criteria

| # | Given | When | Then |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AC-1** | A driver in California completes a delivery | They view their earnings | They see base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips (separate line), deductions (itemized), and net pay — all mandatory, no opt-out |
| **AC-2** | A driver in Germany exists on the platform | Works council approval is NOT recorded | The earnings breakdown feature is completely hidden/disabled for all DE drivers |
| **AC-3** | A driver in Germany exists AND works council approval IS recorded | They complete a delivery | Full earnings breakdown is shown, compliant with approved format |
| **AC-4** | A driver in Indonesia completes a delivery | They view their earnings | Breakdown is shown with figures matching their contractor agreement terms |
| **AC-5** | Any driver views an earnings breakdown | Deductions exist | Each deduction is a separate line item with reason and authorization — no lump sums |
| **AC-6** | A CA driver's earnings are calculated | Tips are received | Tips appear as a separate line item AND base pay amount is identical whether tips are $0 or $1000 |
| **AC-7** | A finalized pay period exists | A correction is needed | Original records are unchanged; a new adjustment line item appears |
| **AC-8** | Any earnings breakdown is displayed | The arithmetic is checked | `net_earnings == gross_earnings - sum(deductions)` exactly, no rounding drift |

**Edge Cases:**

| Scenario | Expected Behavior |
|---|---|
| Driver operates in multiple jurisdictions (e.g., cross-border DE/NL) | Classification and rules are determined by the jurisdiction of each individual delivery, not the driver's "home" jurisdiction |
| Works council rejects the proposed format in Germany | Feature remains disabled in DE; platform must negotiate revised format — no partial rollout or workaround |
| A delivery is disputed/cancelled after earnings are shown | Original earnings record is immutable; a reversal/adjustment line item is appended |
| Tip is modified after initial display (customer adjusts tip) | Tip adjustment appears as a new line item; original tip record is preserved |
| Zero-deduction delivery | Breakdown still shows deductions section with $0.00 total — section is not hidden |
| Minimum wage shortfall detected in CA | System must flag and auto-correct (top-up) before displaying to driver; must never show an earnings breakdown that implies sub-minimum-wage payment |

---

### Confidence Assessment

- **Confidence Level: 82%**

**High confidence areas:**
- California wage transparency and tip-separation rules (well-established law, AB5 widely documented)
- Works council co-determination rights in Germany (clear legal framework)
- Arithmetic integrity of earnings breakdown (universal domain rule)
- Immutability of finalized records (standard financial domain pattern)

**Low confidence areas:**
- Indonesian contractor law specifics — while transparency isn't required, there may be tax reporting or withholding display requirements not captured here
- Cross-border delivery scenarios — driver classification in edge cases (e.g., a German driver doing a delivery that starts in Netherlands) needs jurisdiction-specific legal guidance
- Whether Germany's works council approval must cover the data model/schema or only the UI presentation
- Specific California itemization requirements beyond what's covered (e.g., mileage reimbursement display obligations under Labor Code §2802)

**Assumptions:**
- Worker classification has already been correctly determined per jurisdiction and is available as structured data in the system
- The payroll system can provide real-time (sub-5-minute) earnings data, not just batch
- "Real time" means near-real-time (minutes), not sub-second — this is a financial display, not a trading system
- Works council engagement for Germany has a defined internal process with a binary approval status
- The platform's existing currency handling correctly maps jurisdictions to local currency

---

### Rejection Criteria

Any implementation that exhibits these behaviors **must be rejected**:

| Violation | Business Rule Broken |
|---|---|
| Single global feature flag that enables/disables transparency for all regions simultaneously | BR-3, BR-7 — Germany cannot go live without works council approval; jurisdiction rules are independent |
| Tips included in "total compensation" used for minimum wage calculation | BR-1 — Direct violation of California labor law |
| Deductions shown as a single lump sum | BR-4 — Each deduction must be individually itemized |
| Worker classification defaulting to "contractor" when not explicitly set | BR-7 — Classification must be explicit, never inferred |
| Earnings records modified in place for corrections | BR-10 — Corrections must be append-only adjustment line items |
| Feature deployed to Germany without a recorded works council approval | BR-3 — Immediate legal violation of BetrVG §87 co-determination rights |

---

**Bottom line:** This is not a "show some numbers" feature. It is a **jurisdiction-aware, legally regulated compensation disclosure system**. The architecture must treat jurisdiction as a first-class domain concept that determines business rules, not a cosmetic localization concern. Ship it wrong in California and you face class-action lawsuits. Ship it wrong in Germany and you face injunctions. The domain demands correctness — not just functionality.
